title: GDPR Privacy Settings Default Validation (Privacy by Design / Default)
exclusions: Enterprise administrator configurations per organisational DPA that override individual defaults; accessibility accommodations requiring expanded data display; explicit consent-upgraded profiles.
evidence: Privacy settings default validation report showing application audit results, non-compliant defaults identified, remediation ticket references, and re-validation confirmation.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.25(2) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.25(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.45: GDPR Privacy Settings Default Validation (Privacy by Design / Default).
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.45' running on index=app_config (HEC from feature flag / admin APIs), index=github (audit of default config templates), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Deploy a test application instance with privacy-related settings (data sharing, telemetry, profiling) enabled by default rather than disabled; verify the validation check identifies the non-compliant default configuration.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Deploy a test application instance with all privacy settings defaulting to the most protective option (data sharing off, telemetry off, profiling disabled); verify no default validation alert fires.

dataSources: `index=app_config` (HEC from feature flag / admin APIs), `index=github` (audit of default config templates)
app: HTTP Event Collector (HEC), Splunk Add-on for GitHub (Splunkbase 5596)
spl:
index=app_config sourcetype="feature_flag_audit" earliest=-24h
| where match(lower(change_type),"(?i)default") AND match(lower(flag_name),"(?i)market|track|share|personal")
| eval compliant_default=if(match(lower(new_value),"(?i)off|false|opt.in|disabled"),1,0)
| where compliant_default=0
| stats latest(_time) as last_change latest(new_value) as new_value values(user) as changed_by by app_name flag_name
| table app_name, flag_name, new_value, changed_by, last_change
description: Article 25(2) requires data protection by default — only personal data necessary for each purpose should be processed, with privacy-friendly defaults. This use case audits application configuration events and feature-flag stores for marketing, analytics, and profile-sharing defaults that are not opt-in where required.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes app_config, github and sourcetypes feature_flag_audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Instrument admin and feature-flag systems to emit changes via HEC; (2) define per-product policy for safe defaults; (3) alert product owners on non-compliant default changes before release; (4) pair with code review sampling from GitHub default config files; (5) document evidence for Art. 25 DPIAs. Indexes required: `index=app_config`, `index=github`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=feature_flag_audit`.
visualization: Table (non-compliant defaults), Timeline (changes), Single value (violations), Bar chart (by product line). Key fields: `app_name`, `changed_by`, `compliant_default`, `flag_name`, `last_change`, `new_value`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Feature flags for progressive rollout** — New features deployed with privacy-protective defaults but tested with expanded settings for beta users who explicitly opted in may show non-default configurations.

2. **Platform update default resets** — System updates that temporarily display reset defaults before user preferences are loaded from the profile store may appear as non-default settings during the brief loading window.

3. **Admin configuration for organisational accounts** — Enterprise administrators configuring tenant-wide settings that override individual defaults per the organisational data processing agreement.

4. **Accessibility accommodations** — Privacy settings adjusted for accessibility requirements (e.g., larger fonts requiring additional data display) that technically expand data visibility.

5. **Consent-upgraded profiles** — Users who have explicitly consented to additional processing through the consent management platform have legitimately non-default settings.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `app_config` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.25(2)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.25(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.45: GDPR Privacy Settings Default Validation (Privacy by Design / Default)." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `app_config` under `feature_flag_audit`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Instrument admin and feature-flag systems to emit changes via HEC. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** define per-product policy for safe defaults. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert product owners on non-compliant default changes before release. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** pair with code review sampling from GitHub default config files. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** document evidence for Art. 25 DPIAs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `latest()`, `values()` across the last 24 hours, grouped by `app_name, flag_name`. For "GDPR Privacy Settings Default Validation (Privacy by Design / Default)," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. `values()` collects distinct values into a multivalue field per group; `list()` preserves order and duplicates. For compliance evidence, `values()` is preferred — the auditor cares what values appeared, not how many times. `earliest()` and `latest()` extract the first and last value per group — typically used to measure durations ('first seen' to 'last seen') or to pick the freshest attribute value when multiple events update the same entity.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `app_name, flag_name` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(app_name) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.45: GDPR Privacy Settings Default Validation (Privacy by Design ]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.45,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=app_config sourcetype=feature_flag_audit earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `app_name`, `changed_by`, `compliant_default`, `flag_name`, `last_change`, `new_value`. Run:

```spl
index=app_config sourcetype=feature_flag_audit earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table app_name, changed_by, compliant_default, flag_name, last_change, new_value
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.45: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`non-compliant defaults`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Time trend (`changes`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`violations`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 4 — Distribution (`by product line`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of privacy settings default validation (privacy by design / default) — so we can show we look after private information the way the privacy rules that apply to us require.
